Hey guys, JB is in the house. Time for your weekly intensive and we are rocking and rolling here. We are on week number four of our cycle from the people who are signed up for the certification program and the number grows every week. Mathly, which is awesome. So please pay special attention to the exercises now going forward, because these start to get really crucial in terms of being building blocks. All right. So that being said, tonality. If there's one module that I would say was a linchpin on the front end of the straight line before you ask, it would have to be tonality, right? This is the one where even people who hate me, people like would say, they say, wow, that's like just something about the way I crack the code on tonality that just makes a massive difference. And just so you know, you know, tonality is one of those things that I didn't realize myself how powerful it was and how on target my tonality was until the meat business. When I started in the meat business, that was the first time I started training salespeople. And I started noticing that when other people would speak, they just didn't sound good. They just, they sounded flat. They sounded bland. They didn't seem excited about what they were selling. They didn't seem to believe in what they were selling. They didn't sound certain about what they were selling. And those are all not, those are quite one tonality, but I just noticed that when I was out in the field, the impact it would have on people when I would speak a certain way. And I realized very quickly that I was able to use tonality in conjunction with body language out in the field, right? To get people to just simply open up and say, sure, what do you got? Because when I was in the meat business, there was really very blunt force pitch. It wasn't like alien, right? Here's what we would do. We'd start off, I'd just knock on someone's door and say, Hey, my name is Jordan. I deliver meat and seafood to some of your neighbors in the area. I have some extra boxes on the truck. I'll give you a really good deal. That was it. And they would say, not interested or what do you got? That was it was not interested or what do you got? Right? Well, for some reason, when I was knocking on people's doors, when they were huge mansions, or small homes, or just walking into a real estate office, I go to businesses too, right? For some reason, I could go down one side of the street or one row of homes while my partner for the day, I had your truck partners, I was trading people or just, you know, some of us wanted company, right? You go down one side, I go down the other right. I go down one side of the street and I would sell a ton of boxes of meat. And someone would go down the other side of the street and they couldn't sell a thing. They couldn't even get a pitch off. And it kind of baffled me in the beginning. And then I think it really crystallized itself even after Stratton, when I was in the mortgage industry and I went out business to business for refinancing, right? And I came up with this idea. Let me just go out there into the field door to door, right? So I actually went with my partner's wife. She walked down one side of the street. I walked down the other side of the street and I had made about $35,000 before I even made it past one block. I grossed $35,000 in commission on the first block, the first doors I hit, right? And I was like, oh my God, I've cracked the code. This is the most amazing thing ever. And I figured that when I got to the other side of the street, she'd be like jumping for joy as well. I think it was Shelly, right? It's a true story, right? Maybe she's listening. And when I got to the other side of the street, I saw Shelly sitting on a park bench crying. She's like, I'm like, Shelly, what happened? She says, nobody wants and they threw me out. I was like, what? I just made $35,000. She's like, what? I'm like, yeah. She goes, no one was interested. They wouldn't even let me in the stores. I said, come on. She goes, I swear. I showed her my applications. I said, follow me. And I went back down the same row of stores that she had just been down. I'm a mortgage broker in the area. What rate you're paying in your home right now? If you're paying more than 8%, I could save you a ton of money. That was the long version, right? But I hear the noise in the background. Okay? Like guys, type it away. I'm going to kill them. Anyway, the point is, is that you can't do one without the other. In other words, by making those 200 and 300 calls a day, you get on a roll. Your energy level stays high. Part of tonality. What, well, let's, let's, let's get specific on the tonalities. What essentially is happening is that tonality allows you to transfer energy over the phone. That's what's happening. The tonalities that you use, it doesn't matter what they are, reasonable man, certainty, phrasing it to clarify. The point is, is energy is flowing out of you over the phone line into the ear of the person and causing them to want to listen to your next syllable. That's what's going on. You know, on some level, they say, you know, what's the definition of marketing? Well, marketing is all about selling the next step. What's the next step in the process? That's marketing is making people take the next step. Whatever that might be. Well, in this case, when it comes to tonality, when you, that initial pop on the phone, it's all about just getting people to take the next step. And that is just all you're thinking about is trying to get a positive open greeting from them. You can't think past that. I don't know what's going to happen. They might qualify. They might hate what I have to offer. I can't control that. What I can control in a very big way is my ability in those first couple of seconds to make an emotional connection with someone. So they just think, wow, this is not the average person I'm speaking to. They sound different. This is what some of tonality is about sounding different, sounding unique, sounding good, sounding on target, allowing the energy that's inside of you to transfer over a very long distance to someone you've never seen before. Think about that. You're transferring energy to someone that could be a thousand or five thousand or half a world away. That's powerful. Doesn't happen with words. Body language is marginally effective for that. Really, it is. It's your tonality. So I need you the first step in this because I'm honestly not going to spend 50 minutes training you on specific tonalities. I can't do better than the way I explained it in this module. It was weeks and weeks of work and taping and retaping and probably a thousand hours of graphics in there to give you the perfect mouth strap. So it's not about me saying, okay, let's go through the 10 tonalities. Let me explain what scarcity is. If you don't know, you've got a really big problem. By the way, all right? So that's not what this is about. What I am here to do is tell you, you've got to master this right now. No ifs, ands, or buts. It's time to, you know, draw the line in the sand. Are you going to learn this stuff at the truly highest level or are you going to continue to dabble, be a dabble or an influence, not become a true expert? It starts in those first few seconds. There's a fundamental difference, a radical difference between saying, hi, John, please, hey, John, please, even that. Hey, John, Jordan Belfort. Hi, John, Jordan Belfort. Even the whole idea of declarative versus, hey, John, Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ company, that's phrasing a declarative as a question, right? Hey, John, Jordan Belfort, calling from XYZ. How are you doing today? That's abiding by the so-called rules of tonality, but that is an entirely different thing. Say, hey, John, Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ company is something in my voice. I just, I wish I could explain to you differently. There's some intangible, there's an energy flow that comes out of you, that's palpable, and when people hear it, it makes them want to engage, plain and simple. It's a certain self-belief. It's almost like tied to every tonality. So here's a good way to explain. I'm always trying to go like different ways, right? Because I really think that the tonality module is so tight, like all the basics are covered, right? If you go through it 10 times, you can just keep going through it again and again. There's some wraparound tonality to every other tonality. Like all the tonalities have a wraparound tonality and that wraparound tonality is the tonality of utter, complete, unequivocal self-belief. Self-belief that they should be talking to you and if they don't, they're going to miss out. Yet out of their minds for not wanting to talk to you is a certain self-belief and a conviction in every single utterance that comes out of you. It's a wraparound tonality that comes with all the others. It's what makes scarcity, not John, reason for the call. It's John, reason for the call. It's an oomph of power and I'm not quite sure if most people are really getting this. Like I say, there's pace, pace lead and it's pace, pace freaking lead. One works, one doesn't. One is like, yeah, pace, pace lead, there was ninja style on steroids. You need to take tonality and become a tonal ninja. You just do. That's what I did at Shred. There's an old Tring and you know, I was good at Matt, had a good idea. He goes, why don't you take this guy? We have this kid, George, who's doing very, very well. His tonality is horrendous and he's still doing really well. Not the top of sale in the world. The point is, is that he just want you to show them how you train George. I said, no, I don't want to do that because I really want to train George the right way and I want you guys to see how I really train people in tonality. I'm going to actually take George and other people here through this total training and let you watch it. I'll take it to you and show you what I do. Let me tell you the short story of what I did at Stratton. What I did at Stratton and many plows at the beginning and pretty much everywhere I've ever been after is I would make sure, I would insist that every single salesman could say the first two sentences perfectly. And I mean perfectly as good as me. That was it. And it started there. At Stratton, we'd pass around the mic, pass it around. You have to say your first sentence and if one syllable was off by like one frequency, I'd stop you and make you do it again. And I made it funny, we joke, we laugh, we torture people, but I made every single person actually become 100% proficient expert level at saying two sentences. That's crazy, right? It's not. From those two sentences, all great things would flow. You have to understand guys, there's me walking down the street selling mortgages and there was Shelley. Shelley didn't sound very pretty girl by the way. So it was like my body language gave me an edge. Shelley had the body language. She was a very attractive woman dressed appropriately. Okay, holding the same clipboard. I was also the same like, you know, the binder, right? It wasn't body language. It was the energy that I exuded in the form of self belief that wrapped around every utterance that came out of my mouth. I'm right and you're wrong if you don't want to talk it. I'm in the right in terms of you should want to talk to me. And the way you get that is by ringing the towel dry from that last 2%, the last 1% of sounding awesome. You could sound 98% awesome or you could sound 100% dead on balls accurate and then not the same. They're just not. Now, many of you are on the corporate training side, right? There's a lot of that. It's really expanding rapidly right now. And there'll probably be 98% of my business within the next 3 months, right? The way it's going, right? People will sign up their companies. I want you as companies to go company by company. And I want you guys to break up into groups of 2. Have an A and a B. And I want you guys to make it a point to drill back and forth back and forth back until it's your drill to perfection and do not stop. Do not stop until everybody in your office, no man left behind can say the first couple of sentences of what they got to say when they pick up when they, you know, when the song picks the phone. That's where this has to start. If you want to really use this at the highest level, it's 2 sentences. Once you get those first 2 sentences exactly right, then you want to the next 2 sentences. But you know what? Your 4 or 5 sentences in, guess what's happened? You've used almost every tonality you're going to use and also the shift from like staccato like beats to words that are spaced out. Raise your voice, lower your voice, speed up, slow down. By the time you're literally a few sentences in, if you really mastered that, you know pretty much everything you need to know to become a tonal wizard. That's it. So you have to make the commitment, it's simply a commitment to master this. Know ifs, ands, or buts. Start with the first sentence, which is going to be what? Phrasing your declarative as a question. And then the I really want to know tonality. Hey John, Jordan Belfort, X, Y, Z company in New York. How you doing today? I really want to know. Just start with that. And then from there, you're going to go into mystery and intrigue now. Now John, you might have heard. I mean, I'm just telling you, by the time you're there, just so you know, 75% of you will have already lost. You've already lost the game because you didn't do it right. I'm serious. If you can just please humor me and become a stone cold expert for these first few sentences, I almost as close as I could to give me you a guarantee. You'll see the impact on your business will be absolutely staggering. The first four seconds of the conversation is everything. So remember, right? I said to you that, okay, so what's happening essentially is that when you start that conversation, they're like, oh, what are you doing? You're interrupting someone, right? You're an intrusion on their life. All right. Most people, and this is the fact, walk around or spend at least 80, 90 plus percent of their day in a disempowered state. You know that? You know that? That in the morning, I get up every single morning at 5am. I wake up at 5am and I go to bed at midnight. All right? Sometimes if I have to, I mean, I crash out at 11.30, but I'm up every morning religiously at five o'clock in the morning. And then I lie in bed and I work. Do you know that by 5.45, I am so utterly tired that I would swear that I couldn't get out. I would have, I gotta sleep eight hours more. You know that? I wake up at 5am, like a tiger, at 5.45 when it's now getting light, I am like, I'm like, what happened? Like what happened? Right? Do you know that if I actually get out of bed and start doing something and walking around, all right, lift weights or play tennis or just start actively moving, I feel perfect. My state changes. Most people move through the world in the state as if they're lying down. They're like zombified. Most people you call are feeling like I felt at every morning at 5.35. And the reason I don't jump out of bed is I have a bad back. So a lot of them always wake up at my back, certain a bit, I want to like ease into it seriously. Or else I jump out every morning. I gotta make my way out, right? But I started noticing profoundly that I would get really tired. Yet I couldn't fall back asleep. And I get miserable. And I am, I'm too tired to work, but not tired enough to sleep. And yet if I just simply get out of bed, put my sneakers on, brush my teeth, put it right, I actually start my day. By the time I make it downstairs, I feel perfect. It's like, what happened? I thought I was, I needed sleep. I didn't. Your body tricks you. Why is that important for turnout? I'll tell you why. Because most people walk around in that artificially negative depressed state all day long. You might not be in a state of depression, but they're not at the highest level moving and grooving. I promise you they're not. What I taught my guys back in the day that was such a breakthrough, I remember the word, I said, guys, you're calling people, they're living in these humdrums. Even though they're rich, they're just living these average lives. They just, you need to be that pop, that, that welcome intrusion. You want to be the welcome in the voice that wakes them up, the voice that makes them smile, the energy that comes through and changes their state. If they're in a neutral or disempowered state and you come in just sounding great, not overly stupid. I'm talking that bottle of enthusiasm, that energy. And also is that wrap around tonality of self belief that is infectious. It impacts people. It makes them want to hear more. It makes them want to be in your sphere of influence. It feels good to people. You know, when I do a live event, but it's really, we're getting a scene installed here. It's really hot. So I'm dying right now. All right. But I'm still Rome. Okay. Anyways, here's the deal. When I do a live event, I make everybody stand up and I do an exercise. It's a greeting exercise. They say, all right, I want everybody to walk around the room, just stand up, right? And just, I want you to greet like 10 or 15 people just in your general area, right? Walk around and just give the average neutral green. Say, hi, my name is so-and-so. Nice to meet you. Hi, nice to meet you. Nice. Just a neutral average green the way you would typically do it. All right. I let them go for like 30 seconds doing that. I say, all right, guys, stop. Everyone stops. So they're right. Now, I want you to do the same thing, except I want you to greet someone as if you couldn't care less about them. Like you're totally disinterested. All right. So, and by the way, I'll say, and when I'll say to them, they say, guys, how did that, they tell them to do the neutral ones. I had that feeling. I was like, oh, it was all right, whatever. Just seemed okay. Right. I say, now I want you to do it differently. I want you to say the same words, but I want you to use dismissive body language and how you doing? Okay. And dismissive tonality as if you couldn't care less about this person and do that for another 10 or 15 people. I say, go. And for 30 seconds, a minute, they go around the room. The room's crazy. I say, stop. I say, let me ask you a question. How many of you felt better giving that greeting? No one raises hand. How many of you felt worse giving that greeting? Everyone raised, everyone hates giving that dismissive downbeat greeting. Every person raises their hand. I said, okay, now when you're on the receiving end, let me ask you a question. How many of you liked receiving that greeting more than the other? No one raises your hand. I said, how many of you just hated that greeting and wanted no part of the person? Every single person raises their hand. Okay. I said, okay, great. That's our baseline. Now what I want you to do is I want you to do this again, except now when I tell you go, I want you to use the same words, but just really go out and say, hey, nice to meet you with a big smile, like a really happy, like just, you know, really positive upbeat greeting and do that for 30 seconds. I said, go. The room is much louder now. It's more animated. Everyone's smiling. They go around. Minute later, I say, stop. I said, let me ask you guys a question. Who liked giving that greeting the most so far? Everyone's like, yeah. I said, okay, now who liked getting that greeting the most? Everyone's like, yeah. Everyone's like, okay, great. So lesson learned, right? The one we like giving the most is the positive upbeat greeting, right? The one we hate the most is the negative sort of, I don't care greeting. Let's do this one more time now. I said, I want you to do the same thing except this time. I want you to greet the person like they are your long lost best friend in the world. Like that you haven't seen him for years. You're just crazy. Oh my God. You want to just jump all of your joy to hug them, almost kiss them. Don't kiss them. But just go wild, super enthusiastic. That being said, a big part of that is your use of tonality to sound like an expert. Remember, there's two types of people in the world. There are those that sound like experts and those that don't. The ones that sound like experts are the ones who get paid. Unfortunately, within that group of sound like experts are people who really are experts and who don't. They're faking it. I don't want you to be in the group of fakers, but I want you to actually use tonality, use body language, use the straight line to sound like an expert. Any ideas? Harry, I'm going to cover this in the follow-up, right? But here's some proof of thought. I struggled with this in the mortgage industry, right? Because, you know, we're using a two-call system where we'd set phone appointments and then follow-up in person. That was, we had other divisions doing just phone, but that was a big part. You set appointments by phone file in person. And the question is, do you call to confirm or do you just show up? Well, what I would find, just, you know, my, my two cents here, one side of the equation, Harry, is that if you have an appointment with someone and you don't confirm it and you show up and they're not there, that really sucks or doesn't. That person feels so bad when they find out you at their home or their business that your closing rate for that person goes flying up. I've tested this, okay? See, when you call someone back right before to confirm, you know, you get this, people say, oh, no, no, I can't, you get it. So there's, and then there's a flip side though, do you want to go 300 miles away or 200 miles and not confirm? So you got to be smart about it. There is an equation here. It's not, is it an equation about a, do I confirm an appointment and the equation is based on how far away is the appointment? How many other points do I have in the area if that appointment cancels? And then testing this idea for those, because there's going to be a lot of cancellations. It won't be there. But watch what happens when you show up there and you leave your card or whatever and they find out, they feel so terrible that your closing rate goes up by at least 50% or better. So you want to test that out, test confirming versus not confirming, okay? One of the big things I always do is, you know, it's like, you know, I never would say to someone, oh, I'm gonna, oh, you're over at Brooklyn, great. I'm happy to come take a special trip to Brooklyn to see you and discuss this in more detail. I'm like, oh, you're over at Brooklyn. Oh, I'm gonna be in the area next week on Tuesday and Wednesday. I'm already gonna be in the area. Which day is better for you? In other words, when I set my appointments, I always say, this is a whole different thing. We're going to go from appointment setting, right? But the point is, is that you're going to get cancellations, all right? And the mistake that people make is if you try too hard to confirm and lock it in, you don't give yourself that chance of them not being there and then them definitely buying on the next round. You got to test this, Harry, okay? And because if it, because if you find out that by not confirming your cancellation rate goes up 20% but your closing goes up 50% in the back end, it'll be the best move you ever made.
